original_abstract:
Specific effects of electrolytes have posed a central challenge to physical chemistry since the late 1880s. This field was pioneered by Franz Hofmeister, who first quantified the ability of different salts to induce protein precipitation. Far from being anomalies, these ion-specific effects are the rule rather than the exception, appearing ubiquitously across chemistry and biology. Despite their importance, conventional electrostatic frameworks—such as Debye-Hückel and DLVO theories—fail to explain these phenomena. Over the past several decades, it has become clear that standard theories omit crucial quantum mechanical dispersion forces and the associated hydration effects that act specifically on different ions.

In parallel with theoretical developments, Collins proposed a phenomenological framework known as the Law of Matching Water Affinities (LMWA). This system uses the concept of "kosmotropes" and "chaotropes" to bring qualitative order to ion–ion and ion–surface interactions. While successful in a practical sense, the LMWA and the quantum mechanical approach often appear to be at odds. Although the necessity of including dispersion forces is now widely accepted, early modeling attempts were frequently oversimplified, failing to capture the unique chemical nature of the interacting species—a strength that the LMWA possesses.

In this review, we explore how these two seemingly opposing perspectives can be reconciled. By incorporating ab initio quantum mechanics to quantify ion size, polarizability, and hydration, we move toward a unified theory that provides both physical rigor and predictive power. However, significant challenges remain. The role of dissolved gases, the impact of "hydrophobic" interactions, and the reorganization of water structure at varied temperatures suggest a complexity that may eventually require a complete rebuilding of our theoretical foundations.
humanized_abstract:
Electrolyte effects have been a major challenge to physical chemistry since late 19th century, when the effects were discovered by Franz Hofmeister and later quantified in terms of the Hofmeister series describing the salt-induced precipitation of proteins. It is now clear that ion specific effects are not exceptions to the rule but the rule itself, occurring throughout chemistry and biology. These biological effects are generally not explained by conventional electrostatic models such as Debye-Hückel or DLVO theory. They occur in systems where these theories are not applicable and are the result of omission of numerous important ion-specific quantum mechanical effects arising from dispersion forces and hydration. These effects have been omitted from modeling electrolyte effects for decades.

Around the same time as the theoretical advance by Collins, a phenomenological theory known as the Law of Matching Water Affinities (LMWA) was proposed by Collins. The LMWA together with the related concepts of kosmotropes and chaotropes bring qualitative order to ion–ion and ion–surface interactions. In retrospect, the LMWA and the quantum mechanical theory are difficult to reconcile. While it is clear that dispersion forces must play a role in the interactions between ions and surfaces, a number of early attempts to model them were ham-fisted and did not account for the unique chemical character of the species that are involved—and it is precisely this aspect that is embodied in the LMWA.

How to reconcile the ionic versus solvated picture of actinides in solution? The question raised in this review will at least allow us to take the first steps toward a solution to this longstanding issue by incorporating ab initio quantum mechanical calculations to describe in detail the sizes of the ions and their polarizabilities as well as their hydration. A way toward an intellectually more satisfying, rigorous and predictive description is proposed. Nevertheless, important unsolved problems will remain to be solved. They range from the effect of dissolved gases, "hydrophobic" effects or even a description of the influence of water reorganization around the ions according to the temperature, all factors implying such a number of conceptual revisions that one wonders whether the classical view will remain valid or has to be radically changed.
